PARAGRAPH 1
To solve the problem of filtering historical dates by century, we must first understand the mathematical relationship between a calendar year and its corresponding century. A century is a period of one hundred years. The first century began in year one and ended in year one hundred. The second century began in year one hundred one and ended in year two hundred. Following this pattern, the nineteenth century encompasses the years from one thousand eight hundred one through one thousand nine hundred. The twentieth century starts in one thousand nine hundred one and ends in two thousand. This logic is crucial for accurate filtering. Many people mistakenly believe that the year two thousand belongs to the twentieth century, but it actually marks the start of the twenty-first century. Therefore, when a user requests dates for the nineteenth century, the code must check if the year falls between one thousand eight hundred one and one thousand nine hundred inclusive.

The Python function will accept a list of strings formatted as year dash month dash day. It will also accept an integer representing the target century. The core logic involves extracting the year from each date string. We can achieve this by splitting the string at the dash character and taking the first element. This element is then converted from a text string to an integer. Once we have the integer year, we calculate the start and end years for the requested century. The start year is calculated by taking the century number, subtracting one, multiplying by one hundred, and adding one. The end year is simply the century number multiplied by one hundred. For example, if the century is nineteen, the start is one thousand eight hundred one and the end is one thousand nine hundred.

We then iterate through every date in the provided list. For each date, we perform the year extraction and comparison. If the year is greater than or equal to the start year and less than or equal to the end year, we add that date string to our result list. This approach is efficient because it processes each item exactly once. The time complexity is linear relative to the number of dates in the input list. This makes it suitable for handling very large datasets without significant performance degradation. We do not need to import heavy libraries like pandas for this specific task, as standard Python string manipulation and integer arithmetic are sufficient and fast.
